text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're talking about one of my favorite applications of robotic and MIS approaches and that's in the management of patients with subxiphoid hernias.

This was a four-year-old man with a history of aortopathy related to their connective tissue disease. a history of NUS procedure for pectus abnormality and aortic root reconstruction via an extended sternotomy.
CT scan images show herniation of preperitoneal and falciform fat through the lower aspect of the sternotomy incision, visibly protruding through the abdominal wall fascia.
The hernia defect is visually small but contains a large volume of herniated fat. The hernia sac extends approximately ten centimeters from the inferior aspect of the sternum xiphoid region.

As we thought about how to address this, I felt that robotics would be a great way to approach this. We planned for a lower doc, with an anticipated distance of about fifteen centimeters from the inferior aspect of the defect. A trans-abdominal preperitoneal repair with a goal to fully dissect to the central tendon of the diaphragm, evaluate for any diaphragmatic defects, and then aim to use heavyweight mesh to reinforce the eventual defect closure.
The surgeon begins the preperitoneal dissection in the falciform region, revealing herniated contents and part of the defect.

I always gain access in the upper abdomen and then strategically place my ports. Upon starting this operation, it became pretty obvious to me that I could have docked and placed my trochars lower down
The surgeon performs a standard pre-peritoneal dissection, reaching the area of the hernias where fat is visible. The scrub nurse applies manual pressure to assist in reducing the herniated contents.
The surgeon addresses multiple hernia defects, focusing on the reduction of large fat-containing hernias. The herniated contents are gradually reduced, revealing the multifocal nature of the defects.
The surgeon identifies and addresses the first defect along the midline, then proceeds to locate multiple additional defects in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs a standard dissection, aggressively reducing the herniated fat with minimal adhesions visible, facilitating easy reduction.
The surgeon fully dissects and reduces the incarcerated contents near the xiphoid process, preparing to develop a healthy space for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully checks for any viscera near the surgical site before proceeding with subdiaphragmatic dissection. The central tendon of the diaphragm becomes visible as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon uses cautery to address small bleeders while continuing to identify the central tendon of the diaphragm during the subdiaphragmatic dissection.
The final view shows approximately seven centimeters from the superior aspect of the defect, indicating the area for mesh coverage. The surgeon prepares to close the defect.
The surgeon addresses the subxiphoid hernia by closing the smaller defect transversely, followed by a vertical closure of the upper defect, demonstrating strategic re-approximation of the fascia.

Tissue came together nicely. No major issues. No need for any myofascial releases. And I always tell those that operate with me that the benefits of a TAR or myofascial advancement in this region of the abdominal wall is minimal. And so I don't feel that it's absolutely necessary, even if there is tension.

The surgeon places a heavyweight mesh measuring fifteen by eighteen centimeters over the atypical hernia site, ensuring circumferential coverage around the small defects. The mesh is positioned to reinforce the area effectively.

The surgeon completes fixation with absorbable sutures and proceeds with a straightforward peritoneal closure. The camera is positioned close to the surgical field, requiring careful maneuvering to avoid collisions.

This patient did have aortic root reconstruction and was taking high dose aspirin at baseline. We transitioned them to prior to the operation and had them continue with baby dose aspirin for about a week after surgery.

I did not place a grain as the operation was quite hemostatic. You see just the sheer volume of that pre-peritoneal fat that we eventually reduced. Same day discharge and the patient had an uneventful course. Hopefully this was a helpful video. Thank you.
